title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Great Wall of China. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Dragon’s Backbone: Myths of the Great Wall
content.text: The Great Wall of China is often described as the only man-made structure visible from space—a claim that is, in fact, a persistent urban legend. Despite this bit of astronomical fiction, the wall remains one of the most staggering engineering feats in human history. It is not a single, continuous line, but a complex labyrinth of walls, trenches, and natural barriers stretching over 21,000 kilometers. ||(41)|| [41]This early phase of construction was characterized by 'rammed earth' techniques rather than the iconic gray brick we associate with the structure today[/41]. Laborers compressed soil, gravel, and reeds into wooden frames to create formidable barriers against nomadic raids. However, these ancient sections were largely disconnected until the first Emperor, Qin Shi Huang, began the monumental task of linking them into a unified defensive system. ||(42)|| [42]The logistics of such a project were nightmarish[/42]. Millions of workers, including soldiers, peasants, and convicts, were mobilized to work in some of the most inhospitable terrain on Earth. Many lost their lives to exhaustion, famine, and the freezing temperatures of the northern deserts, leading to the grim nickname 'the longest cemetery on Earth.' ||(43)|| [43]Such sacrifices were driven by the constant threat of the Xiongnu and other powerful tribal confederations[/43]. The wall was designed not just as a physical barrier, but as a sophisticated communication network. Watchtowers were positioned at regular intervals, allowing smoke signals by day and lantern fires by night to transmit warnings across the empire at incredible speeds. ||(44)|| [44]This architectural transition occurred during the Ming Dynasty, the era responsible for the most famous and well-preserved sections of the wall[/44]. Using kiln-fired bricks and a surprisingly durable mortar made of sticky rice, Ming engineers created a structure that could withstand both the elements and the weight of heavy artillery. ||(45)|| [45]However, even this massive stone shield could not save the empire from internal collapse[/45]. In 1644, a Ming general opened the gates at Shanhai Pass to the Manchu forces, effectively ending the dynasty and beginning the Qing era. Under this new leadership, the wall's military importance dwindled as the empire’s borders expanded far beyond the stone fortifications. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the Great Wall faces a new set of challenges, from natural erosion to the impact of mass tourism[/46]. While some sections have been meticulously restored for visitors, others are slowly crumbling back into the dust of the Gobi Desert. Preserving this 'Dragon’s Backbone' is now a race against time and the elements.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] the origins of the wall [/41] date back more than two millennia to the Warring States period. Independent kingdoms built separate fortifications to protect their territories from one another long before the idea of a unified China had even taken hold.
paragraphs.F: This [42] unification of the defenses [/42] was intended to mark the northern boundary of the newly established empire. It served as a psychological border as much as a physical one, signaling to the world that the era of warring states had been replaced by a single, centralized power.
paragraphs.D: Despite [43] the staggering human cost [/43], the construction never truly ceased. Over the following centuries, successive dynasties repaired and expanded the fortifications, but it wasn't until the 14th century that the wall began to take on its modern, stone-clad appearance.
paragraphs.C: The [44] introduction of these superior materials [/44] represented a major leap in defensive technology. Unlike the earlier earthworks, which required constant maintenance, the brick walls were nearly impenetrable to the siege engines and nomadic cavalry of the time.
paragraphs.G: Beyond [45] its purely defensive function [/45], the wall also served as a vital trade regulator. It allowed the government to impose duties on goods traveling along the Silk Road and to control the movement of people across the frontier, ensuring that the empire’s economic interests were protected.
paragraphs.B: This [46] shift in strategic focus [/46] meant that the wall was largely abandoned for centuries. It wasn't until the 20th century that it was rediscovered as a symbol of national pride, eventually becoming a UNESCO World Heritage site and a global icon of Chinese resilience.
paragraphs.E: Many [47] modern historians argue [/47] that the wall was actually a failure as a defensive structure. They point out that it was frequently bypassed or ignored by invading armies who found it easier to bribe the guards at the gates than to climb over the battlements.

questions.explanation: A refers to 'the origins of the wall' and the 'Warring States period', which provides the historical context for the 'early phase of construction' described after the gap.
F links the 'unification of the defenses' to Emperor Qin Shi Huang’s task of linking the sections mentioned just before the gap.
D refers to the 'staggering human cost' (the 'longest cemetery' mentioned before) and introduces the transition to the 'modern, stone-clad appearance'.
C discusses 'superior materials' (bricks and sticky rice mortar) which the main text identifies as the hallmarks of the Ming Dynasty sections.
G moves from the 'defensive function' described before the gap to the 'trade regulator' and 'Silk Road' context that fits the broader role of the wall.
B explains the 'shift in strategic focus' following the Manchu invasion and the subsequent abandonment and 20th-century rediscovery of the wall.
